prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 23-year-old Black woman.

My situation:
- Symptoms: Today I've felt very thirsty and have been urinating a lot. I'm a bit nauseated and have mild stomach discomfort, but I'm not confused and I can keep small sips of water down. No fever.
- History: I have type 1 diabetes.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms — extreme thirst, frequent urination, nausea and abdominal discomfort — in the setting of type 1 diabetes strongly suggest high blood sugar and raise concern for early diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA). You are not confused and can sip fluids, which is reassuring, but DKA can develop quickly. You need prompt assessment to check blood glucose and blood or urine ketones, hydration status, and whether your insulin regimen needs adjustment. If ketones are moderate/large, you are vomiting, can’t keep fluids down, develop rapid breathing, confusion, or worsening symptoms, that is an emergency.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms (very thirsty, urinating a lot, nausea, mild stomach discomfort) are common signs of high blood sugar and could be early diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), a complication more likely in type 1 diabetes. Because you are not confused and can keep sips of water down, you may not be in severe DKA right now, but this can develop quickly. Check your blood glucose and urine or blood ketones if you can, take your prescribed insulin as directed, and drink fluids. Seek immediate care if you have persistent vomiting, rapid breathing, fruity breath, confusion, inability to keep fluids down, very high glucose (>300 mg/dL) or moderate/large ketones.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 75%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms (very thirsty, urinating a lot, nausea, mild stomach discomfort) in someone with type 1 diabetes suggest high blood sugar and could be early diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA). Because you’re not confused and can keep small sips, it may be mild now, but DKA can worsen quickly. If you can, check blood glucose and urine or blood ketones right away, drink fluids, and give any usual correction insulin per your plan. Seek care promptly if glucose is very high, ketones are moderate/large, you vomit or can’t keep fluids down, breathing gets fast/deep, you become confused, or symptoms worsen.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 60%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms (very thirsty, peeing a lot, nausea, stomach discomfort) in someone with type 1 diabetes strongly suggest high blood sugar and raise concern for early diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA). Early DKA often causes thirst, frequent urination, nausea, and abdominal pain; it can progress quickly if ketones build up, especially in type 1 diabetes. Because you are not confused and can keep sips of water down, this may be early or moderate rather than severe, but it still needs prompt evaluation. Check your blood glucose and, if available, urine or blood ketones now. If ketones are present, glucose is very high, you vomit, can’t keep fluids down, become short of breath, or confused, you need emergency care.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You have classic signs of high blood sugar (very thirsty, peeing a lot) plus nausea and mild stomach discomfort. Because you have type 1 diabetes, these symptoms can sometimes progress to diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), which is serious. Right now it’s somewhat reassuring that you’re not confused and can keep sips of fluid, but you should check your blood glucose and, if available, blood or urine ketones immediately. Follow your usual correction insulin plan and contact your diabetes care team. Go to the emergency room right away if ketones are present, your glucose is very high and not falling, you start vomiting or can’t keep fluids down, develop rapid breathing, worsening abdominal pain, weakness, or confusion.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms (very thirsty, peeing a lot, nausea, mild stomach discomfort) are classic for high blood sugar and could be the start of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), a complication of type 1 diabetes. Because you are not confused, can sip fluids, and have no fever or vomiting, this may be early hyperglycemia rather than full DKA — but it can progress. If you can, check your blood glucose and urine or blood ketones now. Stay hydrated with small sips, follow your usual insulin correction plan, and avoid sugary drinks. Seek faster care if you have large ketones, very high glucose, increasing nausea/vomiting, rapid breathing, fruity breath, dizziness, or confusion.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your thirst, frequent urination, nausea, and stomach discomfort in the setting of type 1 diabetes most likely mean your blood sugar is high. These are also early signs of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), a condition where, without enough insulin, the body makes acids called ketones. You’re less likely to be in severe DKA right now because you are alert and can keep small sips down, but this can worsen. Check your blood glucose and, if available, urine or blood ketones right away. Drink plain water, avoid sugary drinks, and follow your usual insulin plan or sick-day instructions. If ketones are moderate/large, glucose is very high, or you develop vomiting, rapid breathing, belly pain, or confusion, you need urgent care.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your thirst, frequent urination, nausea, and mild stomach discomfort in the setting of type 1 diabetes most likely indicate high blood sugar and could be early diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA). DKA can begin with these symptoms and can worsen fairly quickly. Check your blood glucose now and, if you can, test urine or blood ketones. Continue sipping fluids and take your usual insulin per your sick-day instructions — do not skip insulin. Seek prompt medical evaluation if ketones are moderate/large, glucose is very high, you vomit or can’t keep fluids down, develop fast breathing, abdominal pain, or mental changes.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 75%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms (very thirsty, urinating a lot, nausea, mild stomach pain) in the setting of type 1 diabetes most commonly mean high blood sugar and could be early diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA). Check your blood glucose now and test for ketones (urine or blood) if you can. Drink sugar-free fluids and follow your usual insulin correction plan if you know it. Go to the emergency room right away if you have large/positive ketones, persistent vomiting, rapid breathing, confusion, or can’t keep fluids down. If glucose is only mildly high and no ketones, contact your diabetes provider promptly for advice.

TRIAGE: C
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You have classic signs of high blood sugar (very thirsty, peeing a lot) and early warning signs that can occur with diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) in type 1 diabetes (nausea, stomach discomfort). This can progress if not treated. Check your blood glucose now and, if possible, test urine or blood ketones. Continue your prescribed insulin (do not stop). Drink small sips of water to avoid dehydration and avoid sugary drinks. If your glucose is very high, ketones are positive, you begin vomiting, have worsening belly pain, fast or deep breathing, fruity breath, or any confusion, you need emergency care immediately.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 75%
